summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is a polyhedron?

Polyhedra A polyhedron (plural: polyhedra) is a solid bounded by polygons called faces. Euler's Formula For any convex polyhedron: V − E + F = 2 , where V = vertices, E = edges, F = faces. The other options — 'Any three-dimensional object', 'A circle in 3D space', and 'A curved surface enclosing a volume' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

What is a prism?

Common 3D Figures Prism : Two congruent, parallel polygonal bases connected by rectangular faces. The other options — 'A solid with one triangular base', 'A solid with no flat faces', and 'A sphere with flat sides' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

What is a cross section?

Cross Sections A cross section is the shape formed when a Plane intersects a solid figure. The other options — 'The bottom face of a polyhedron', 'The outline of a 3D figure', and 'A diagonal of a polyhedron' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

What is the difference between a cylinder and a prism?

A prism has 2 congruent parallel bases connected by rectangular faces; a pyramid has 1 base and triangular faces meeting at an apex. The answer is A cylinder has circular bases; a prism has polygonal bases. The other options — 'They are the same shape', 'A cylinder has more faces', and 'A prism has curved surfaces' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

What is the apex of a cone?

Cone — One circular base tapering to a Point (apex). Pyramid — One polygonal base with triangular faces meeting at a single Point (apex). The other options — 'The circular base', 'The center of the base', and 'The midpoint of the height' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.
